mnemonics.title: DBA Responsibilities
mnemonics.content: DBA = Security + Backup + Tuning + Integrity + Availability + User Management (SBUTAU)

mnemonics.title: Normal Forms Order
mnemonics.content: 1NF: No Repeating Groups | 2NF: No Partial Dependencies | 3NF: No Transitive Dependencies | BCNF: No Key → Non-key Dependencies

acronyms.usage: Person responsible for managing database systems including security, backups, and performance.
Maximum time allowed to restore database operations after a failure.
Maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time.
Storage technology providing fault tolerance and performance for database servers.
Measure of database throughput and performance.
SQL commands for defining database structures: CREATE, ALTER, DROP.
SQL commands for manipulating data: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a bank transfer as a sealed envelope: either delivered completely (Atomic), recipient receives correct amount (Consistent), other transfers don't interfere (Isolation), once delivered it stays delivered (Durable).
Draw a state diagram: Active → Partially Committed → Committed/Aborted with arrows showing how COMMIT/ROLLBACK/FAILURE move the transaction between states.
Think of a book: Shared lock = multiple people reading together (OK), Exclusive lock = one person writing (no one else can read or write).
Imagine filling water containers: Full = fill all containers, Incremental = fill only empty ones since last fill, Differential = fill all since last full.

analogies.analogy: Like a ship's captain's log - records every action taken, used to reconstruct what happened if something goes wrong.
Like a book index - instead of scanning every page, you use the index to jump directly to the information you need.
Two people each holding one item, each waiting for the other's item - neither can proceed without the other giving up first.
Like organizing a library: fiction in one section, reference in another, periodicals separate - reduces duplication and makes finding books easier.
Like having multiple copies of important documents in different locations - if one is destroyed, others still exist.
